question: What are the Five Pillars of Islam?

options.B: Iman, Salah, Sawm, Zakat, Hajj

explanation: The Five Pillars of Islam are: Shahada (declaration of faith), Salah (prayer), Sawm (fasting in Ramadan), Zakat (obligatory charity), and Hajj (pilgrimage to Makkah for those who are able). These form the foundational framework of a Muslim's worship and practice.

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 2, Hadith 7 (Hadith of Ibn Umar)

question: How many chapters (Surahs) are in the Quran?
explanation: The Quran contains exactly 114 Surahs (chapters), ranging in length from the longest, Surah Al-Baqarah with 286 verses, to the shortest, Surah Al-Kawthar with 3 verses. The Surahs are arranged roughly in order of decreasing length, not in the order of revelation.

source.name: Scholarly consensus; Quran
source.reference: Confirmed by ijma (scholarly consensus) of the companions and transmitted generations

question: What is the meaning of the word 'Islam'?

options.B: Submission and surrender to Allah

explanation: The word 'Islam' is derived from the Arabic root 'salama', meaning submission, surrender, and peace. A Muslim is one who submits to the will of Allah. The Quran uses this term to describe the complete submission of one's will to Allah as the essence of true faith.

source.name: Quran, Surah Al-Imran; Surah Al-Baqarah
source.reference: Surah Al-Imran 3:19; Surah Al-Baqarah 2:112

question: In which month of the Islamic calendar do Muslims fast?

options.B: Rajab

explanation: Muslims fast during the month of Ramadan, the ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar. Fasting in Ramadan is one of the Five Pillars of Islam. Muslims abstain from food, drink, and other invalidating acts from the Fajr (dawn) prayer until the Maghrib (sunset) prayer.

source.name: Quran, Surah Al-Baqarah
source.reference: Surah Al-Baqarah 2:183-185

question: What is the Shahada, the Islamic declaration of faith?

options.B: La ilaha illallah, Muhammadur Rasulullah
options.D: Inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un

explanation: The Shahada is 'La ilaha illallah, Muhammadur Rasulullah' — meaning 'There is no god worthy of worship except Allah, and Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah.' It is the first and most fundamental pillar of Islam. Uttering it with sincere belief and understanding is the entry point into Islam.

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari; Sahih Muslim
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 2, Hadith 7; Sahih Muslim Book 1, Hadith 38

question: What is the name of the angel who brought revelation to Prophet Muhammad (SAW)?

options.B: Mikail (AS)

explanation: The angel Jibril (AS), known in English as Gabriel, was the angel of revelation who conveyed the words of Allah to Prophet Muhammad (SAW). He is described in the Quran as 'Ruh al-Qudus' (the Holy Spirit) and 'Ruh al-Amin' (the Trustworthy Spirit). He also appeared to other prophets including Ibrahim, Musa, and Isa (AS).

source.name: Quran, Surah Al-Baqarah; Surah Ash-Shuara
source.reference: Surah Al-Baqarah 2:97; Surah Ash-Shuara 26:193-194

question: In which month and city was the Quran first revealed, and what was the first verse revealed?

options.B: Ramadan in Makkah; 'Iqra bismi rabbikalladhi khalaq' (Surah Al-Alaq)

explanation: The first revelation descended during the month of Ramadan in Makkah, in the Cave of Hira. The first verses revealed were the opening of Surah Al-Alaq: 'Iqra bismi rabbikalladhi khalaq' — meaning 'Read in the name of your Lord who created.' The angel Jibril (AS) delivered this to the Prophet (SAW) who was approximately 40 years old at the time.

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari; Quran Surah Al-Alaq
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 1, Hadith 3; Surah Al-Alaq 96:1

question: What are the Six Articles of Faith (Arkan al-Iman) in Islam?

options.A: Allah, Angels, Books, Prophets, Day of Judgement, Divine Decree
options.B: Allah, Angels, Quran, Muhammad, Paradise, Hellfire
options.C: Allah, Angels, Books, Prophets, Prayer, Fasting
options.D: Tawhid, Angels, Books, Prophets, Resurrection, Accountability

explanation: The Six Articles of Faith are belief in: Allah (His existence and oneness), the Angels, the revealed Books, the Prophets and Messengers, the Day of Judgement, and Divine Decree (Qadar) — both its good and its seemingly difficult aspects. These are established in the famous Hadith of Jibril and confirmed in the Quran.

source.name: Sahih Muslim; Quran Surah Al-Baqarah
source.reference: Sahih Muslim Book 1, Hadith 8 (Hadith of Jibril); Surah Al-Baqarah 2:177

question: What is the Islamic lunar calendar called, and in which year does it begin?

options.A: The Gregorian calendar; begins from the birth of the Prophet (SAW)
options.B: The Hijri calendar; begins from the migration of the Prophet (SAW) from Makkah to Madinah
options.C: The Hijri calendar; begins from the first revelation of the Quran
options.D: The Shamsi calendar; begins from the conquest of Makkah

explanation: The Islamic calendar is called the Hijri calendar, named after the Hijra — the migration of the Prophet (SAW) from Makkah to Madinah in 622 CE. This event marks year 1 AH (Anno Hegirae). The calendar was formally established during the caliphate of Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) and consists of 12 lunar months totalling 354 or 355 days.

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari; historical scholarly consensus
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 56, Hadith 3906; Al-Bidaya wa'l-Nihaya by Ibn Kathir

question: Which Surah of the Quran is known as the 'heart of the Quran' according to authentic hadith?

options.B: Surah Al-Ikhlas

explanation: Surah Ya-Sin is referred to as the 'heart of the Quran' in a hadith narrated by Ma'qil ibn Yasar (RA), in which the Prophet (SAW) said: 'Ya-Sin is the heart of the Quran.' It is the 36th Surah and contains powerful themes of resurrection, divine power, and the truth of prophethood. It is traditionally recited for the dying and the deceased.

source.name: Sunan Abu Dawud; Musnad Ahmad
source.reference: Sunan Abu Dawud Book 20, Hadith 3121; Musnad Ahmad, Hadith 20307

question: What are the four sacred months (Al-Ashhur al-Hurum) in Islam?

options.A: Ramadan, Shawwal, Dhul Qadah, Dhul Hijjah
options.B: Muharram, Rajab, Dhul Qadah, Dhul Hijjah

explanation: The four sacred months are Muharram, Rajab, Dhul Qadah, and Dhul Hijjah. These are explicitly mentioned in the Quran and confirmed in the hadith of the Prophet (SAW) during his Farewell Pilgrimage. Three of these months are consecutive (Dhul Qadah, Dhul Hijjah, Muharram) and one stands alone (Rajab). Sins committed in these months are considered more serious, and acts of worship more rewarding.

source.name: Quran, Surah At-Tawbah; Sahih al-Bukhari
source.reference: Surah At-Tawbah 9:36; Sahih al-Bukhari Book 59, Hadith 4406

question: What is the term for the migration of the Prophet Muhammad (SAW) from Makkah to Madinah?

options.B: Ghazwa

explanation: The Hijra refers to the Prophet Muhammad's (SAW) migration from Makkah to Madinah in 622 CE, undertaken due to the severe persecution of the early Muslim community by the Quraysh. It marks one of the most pivotal events in Islamic history and is so significant that it became the starting point of the Islamic lunar calendar.

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari; Quran Surah Al-Anfal
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 63, Hadith 3906; Surah Al-Anfal 8:30

question: How many years did the revelation of the Quran span, and in how many distinct phases was it revealed?

options.A: 20 years; two phases — Makkan and Medinan
options.B: 25 years; three phases based on the Prophet's age
options.C: 23 years; two phases — Makkan and Medinan
options.D: 30 years; four phases corresponding to the major battles

explanation: The Quran was revealed over approximately 23 years — 13 years in Makkah (from the first revelation until the Hijra) and 10 years in Madinah (from the Hijra until the Prophet's final illness). Makkan verses generally focus on Tawhid, the afterlife, and morality, while Medinan verses tend to address legislation, community affairs, and interfaith relations. This phased revelation (Tanzil) is one of the Quran's unique characteristics.

source.name: Scholarly consensus; Quran Surah Al-Isra
source.reference: Surah Al-Isra 17:106; Imam al-Suyuti, Al-Itqan fi Ulum al-Quran

question: Which Caliph formally ordered the compilation of the Quran into a single written mushaf (manuscript), and why?

options.A: Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA), after the death of the Prophet (SAW)
options.B: Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (RA), after the Battle of Yamama caused the death of many huffaz
options.C: Uthman ibn Affan (RA), to unify the Muslim community around a single dialect
options.D: Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA), to preserve the Quran from foreign influence

explanation: Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (RA), the first Caliph, ordered the first formal compilation of the Quran into a single written manuscript after the Battle of Yamama (633 CE), in which approximately 70 huffaz (memorisers of the Quran) were martyred. Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) urged this initiative fearing further loss of memorisers. Zayd ibn Thabit (RA) led the compilation committee. Later, Uthman (RA) standardised and distributed copies of this mushaf across the Muslim world.

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 66, Hadith 4986

question: What is the concept of Ijaz al-Quran (the inimitability of the Quran) and what is the primary challenge it presents?

options.A: The Quran's inimitability refers to its length; no book has ever matched it in volume
options.B: The Quran challenges all of humanity and jinn to produce even one Surah comparable to it in literary quality, guidance, and divine character
options.C: The Quran's inimitability refers exclusively to its scientific accuracy, which no ancient text has matched
options.D: It refers to the fact that the Quran has never been translated accurately into any other language

explanation: Ijaz al-Quran refers to the miraculous and inimitable nature of the Quran. The Quran itself issues a challenge (tahhaddi) to all of humanity and jinn: if they doubt its divine origin, let them produce even a single Surah comparable to it. This challenge was issued to the most eloquent Arabic speakers of the time — the Quraysh — yet none could meet it. Scholars identify multiple dimensions of this inimitability including linguistic mastery, legislative wisdom, historical accuracy, and spiritual depth.

source.name: Quran, Surah Al-Baqarah; Surah Yunus; Surah Hud
source.reference: Surah Al-Baqarah 2:23-24; Surah Yunus 10:38; Surah Hud 11:13

question: What are the major signs (Ashraat al-Sa'ah al-Kubra) of the Day of Judgement mentioned in authentic hadith?

options.A: The appearance of Imam Mahdi, Dajjal, descent of Isa (AS), Gog and Magog, three major landslides, rising of the sun from the west, the Beast, and the fire
options.B: Nuclear war, famine, widespread earthquakes, the death of all scholars, and total moral collapse
options.C: The conquest of Rome, appearance of the Dajjal, death of Isa (AS), and the final trumpet
options.D: Ten years of drought, disappearance of the Quran, the death of all believers, darkness covering the earth

explanation: The ten major signs of the Day of Judgement, as established in authentic hadith, are: the appearance of the Dajjal (False Messiah), the descent of Prophet Isa (AS), the emergence of Gog and Magog (Ya'juj and Ma'juj), three major landslides (east, west, and Arabian Peninsula), the rising of the sun from the west, the emergence of the Beast (Dabbat al-Ard), a fire driving people to their final gathering place, and smoke (Dukhan). The Imam Mahdi's appearance is referenced in separate hadith as preceding these events.

source.name: Sahih Muslim
source.reference: Sahih Muslim Book 54, Hadith 2901

question: What is the 'Preserved Tablet' (Al-Lawh al-Mahfuz) in Islamic theology and what is its significance?

options.A: The physical copy of the Quran kept in Masjid al-Haram since the time of the Prophet (SAW)
options.B: A divine record in which Allah has written all of creation, events, and decrees from before the creation of the heavens and earth
options.C: The scrolls of deeds maintained by the recording angels Kiraman Katibin for each individual
options.D: The tablets given to Prophet Musa (AS) on Mount Sinai containing the Tawrah

explanation: Al-Lawh al-Mahfuz (the Preserved Tablet) is a foundational concept in Islamic theology referring to the divine record in which Allah inscribed all of His decrees, the entirety of creation, and all that will occur, before the creation of the heavens and earth. The Prophet (SAW) said Allah wrote the decrees of all creation 50,000 years before the heavens and earth were created. It represents the doctrine of Qadar (divine decree) and is explicitly referenced in Surah Al-Buruj.

source.name: Quran, Surah Al-Buruj; Sahih Muslim
source.reference: Surah Al-Buruj 85:22; Sahih Muslim Book 46, Hadith 2653

question: What is the science of 'Mustalah al-Hadith' (hadith terminology) and what does the classification 'Sahih' require according to hadith scholars?

options.A: It is the science of Quranic recitation; Sahih means the recitation has been transmitted by a single reliable narrator
options.B: It is the science of hadith authentication; a Sahih hadith requires an unbroken chain of trustworthy narrators, sound memory, absence of anomaly, and absence of hidden defect
options.C: It is the science of Islamic jurisprudence; Sahih means the ruling has been agreed upon by all four major madhabs
options.D: It is the science of Islamic history; Sahih means the event has been corroborated by non-Muslim historical sources

explanation: Mustalah al-Hadith is the science dedicated to the methodology of evaluating and classifying hadith. For a hadith to be classified as Sahih (authentic), scholars require five conditions: (1) an unbroken chain of narrators (ittisal al-sanad), (2) all narrators must be upright and trustworthy (adalah), (3) all narrators must have precise memory and accuracy (dabt), (4) the hadith must be free from anomaly or contradiction with stronger narrations (adam al-shudhudh), and (5) it must be free from subtle hidden defects (adam al-illah). This rigorous science was developed by early scholars such as Imam al-Shafi'i, Imam al-Bukhari, and Imam Muslim.

source.name: Ibn Salah, Muqaddimah Ibn Salah; Imam al-Nawawi, Taqrib al-Nawawi
source.reference: Muqaddimah Ibn Salah fi Ulum al-Hadith; Imam al-Nawawi, Al-Taqrib wa al-Taysir

question: What is the name of the night journey in which the Prophet (SAW) was taken from Makkah to Jerusalem and then ascended to the heavens?

options.B: Isra wal-Miraj

explanation: The Isra wal-Miraj refers to the miraculous night journey of the Prophet (SAW). The Isra was the horizontal journey from Masjid al-Haram in Makkah to Masjid al-Aqsa in Jerusalem. The Miraj was the subsequent vertical ascent through the seven heavens. During this journey, the five daily prayers were made obligatory upon the Muslim community.

source.name: Quran, Surah Al-Isra; Sahih al-Bukhari
source.reference: Surah Al-Isra 17:1; Sahih al-Bukhari Book 58, Hadith 227

question: What is the Arabic term for the call to prayer?

options.B: Khutbah

explanation: The Adhan is the Islamic call to prayer announced five times a day to summon Muslims to perform Salah. It was established in the first year of Hijra in Madinah after a companion of the Prophet (SAW), Abdullah ibn Zayd, had a vision of the adhan's words. Bilal ibn Rabah (RA) was appointed as the first muadhdhin (caller to prayer).

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari; Sunan Abu Dawud
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 11, Hadith 578; Sunan Abu Dawud Book 2, Hadith 499

question: How many Juz (parts) is the Quran divided into?
explanation: The Quran is divided into 30 equal parts called Juz (plural: Ajza). This division was made to facilitate the recitation of the entire Quran over the course of a month, particularly during Ramadan. Each Juz is further subdivided into two Hizb, and each Hizb into four Rub'.

source.name: Scholarly consensus
source.reference: Ijma of the scholars of Quran sciences; Al-Itqan fi Ulum al-Quran by Imam al-Suyuti

question: Which was the first city in which the Prophet (SAW) established the first Islamic state after the Hijra?

options.B: Taif

explanation: After migrating from Makkah, the Prophet (SAW) established the first Islamic community and state in Madinah (then known as Yathrib). He built Masjid an-Nabawi, established the Constitution of Madinah (Sahifat al-Madinah) creating bonds between the Ansar and Muhajirun, and set up the foundations of the Muslim community.

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari; Ibn Hisham, Sirah
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 63, Hadith 3906; Ibn Hisham, Al-Sirah al-Nabawiyyah Vol. 2

question: Who was the first person to accept Islam?

options.B: Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA)

explanation: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (RA), the beloved wife of the Prophet (SAW), was the first person to accept Islam. When the Prophet (SAW) returned home trembling after the first revelation in the Cave of Hira, it was Khadijah (RA) who comforted him, believed in him immediately, and supported him unconditionally. She is among the four greatest women in Islamic history.

source.name: Sahih al-Bukhari
source.reference: Sahih al-Bukhari Book 1, Hadith 3

question: What is the term for the scholarly consensus in Islamic jurisprudence, considered one of the four primary sources of Islamic law?

options.B: Qiyas

explanation: Ijma refers to the consensus of qualified Islamic scholars on a particular ruling. It is recognised as one of the four primary sources of Islamic law alongside the Quran, Sunnah, and Qiyas (analogical reasoning). The Prophet (SAW) said: 'My Ummah will not unite upon misguidance,' which is cited as the hadith basis for the authority of Ijma.

source.name: Sunan Ibn Majah; Usul al-Fiqh works
source.reference: Sunan Ibn Majah Book 36, Hadith 3950; Al-Risalah by Imam al-Shafi'i

question: What is the name of the Prophet's (SAW) night prayer, which he performed regularly, and what is its virtuous status?

options.A: Salat al-Duha — a sunnah prayer performed mid-morning
options.B: Salat al-Tahajjud — a highly recommended voluntary prayer performed after midnight, praised extensively in the Quran and Sunnah
options.C: Salat al-Witr — the obligatory odd-numbered prayer at the end of the night
options.D: Salat al-Ishraq — a prayer performed just after sunrise

explanation: Salat al-Tahajjud is the voluntary night prayer performed after sleeping and waking in the latter portion of the night. The Quran commands the Prophet (SAW) to observe it and praises those who rise from their beds to worship Allah. It is considered the best voluntary prayer after the fard prayers, and the Prophet (SAW) never abandoned it even while travelling.

source.name: Quran, Surah Al-Isra; Surah As-Sajdah; Sahih Muslim
source.reference: Surah Al-Isra 17:79; Surah As-Sajdah 32:16; Sahih Muslim Book 6, Hadith 1163

question: What is the chain of transmission in hadith called, and what is the significance of its study?

options.A: Matn — the body of the hadith text, studied to detect fabricated content
options.B: Isnad — the chain of narrators transmitting a hadith, the study of which is the backbone of hadith authentication
options.C: Musnad — the collection of hadiths organised by narrator
options.D: Riwayah — the oral tradition of reciting the Quran from memory

explanation: The Isnad is the chain of narrators that traces a hadith back to the Prophet (SAW). Abdullah ibn al-Mubarak famously said: 'The isnad is part of the religion — if not for the isnad, anyone could say anything they wished.' The science of evaluating narrators (Ilm al-Rijal) was developed specifically to verify the isnad, making Islamic hadith scholarship unique among world religious traditions in its rigorous oral transmission methodology.

source.name: Sahih Muslim, Introduction; Ibn al-Mubarak
source.reference: Muqaddimah Sahih Muslim; Ibn al-Mubarak as cited in Sahih Muslim's Introduction

question: What is the concept of Tawhid al-Asma wal-Sifat (the Oneness of Allah's Names and Attributes) and what does it require?

options.A: The belief that Allah has no names and cannot be described in any way
options.B: Affirming Allah's names and attributes as He described Himself and as the Prophet (SAW) described Him, without distortion, denial, likening to creation, or asking how
options.C: The practice of reciting the 99 names of Allah daily as a form of worship
options.D: The scholarly consensus that Allah's attributes are metaphorical and must be reinterpreted philosophically

explanation: Tawhid al-Asma wal-Sifat is the third category of Islamic monotheism, requiring belief in all names and attributes Allah and His Messenger (SAW) affirmed, while avoiding four errors: Ta'til (denial/stripping), Tahrif (distortion of meaning), Tamthil (likening to creation), and Takyif (speculating about the how). The Quran states: 'There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the Hearing, the Seeing' — affirming attributes while negating any resemblance to creation.

source.name: Quran, Surah Ash-Shura; Ibn Taymiyyah, Al-Aqidah al-Wasitiyyah
source.reference: Surah Ash-Shura 42:11; Al-Aqidah al-Wasitiyyah by Ibn Taymiyyah

question: What is the concept of Naskh (abrogation) in Quranic sciences, and which Surah explicitly acknowledges it?

options.A: Naskh refers to the translation of Quranic verses into other languages and is discussed in Surah Al-Baqarah
options.B: Naskh is the replacement or modification of an earlier Quranic ruling by a later one, explicitly acknowledged in Surah Al-Baqarah
options.C: Naskh refers to the physical writing of the Quran and is mentioned in Surah Al-Kahf
options.D: Naskh means the repetition of certain verses in the Quran for emphasis, as noted in Surah Ar-Rahman

explanation: Naskh (abrogation) is the Quranic principle by which a later divine ruling supersedes or modifies an earlier one. Allah states in Surah Al-Baqarah: 'We do not abrogate a verse or cause it to be forgotten except that We bring forth one better than it or similar to it.' A well-known example is the gradual prohibition of alcohol. This field of study (Ilm al-Nasikh wal-Mansukh) is essential for understanding the development of Islamic rulings.

source.name: Quran, Surah Al-Baqarah; Al-Itqan fi Ulum al-Quran
source.reference: Surah Al-Baqarah 2:106; Imam al-Suyuti, Al-Itqan fi Ulum al-Quran

question: According to the famous Hadith of Jibril, what are the three levels of the religion (al-Din)?

options.B: Tawhid, Sunnah, and Fiqh

explanation: In the famous Hadith of Jibril (AS), the angel came to the Prophet (SAW) in the form of a man and asked him about Islam (the outward pillars of practice), Iman (the inward articles of belief), and Ihsan (excellence in worship — to worship Allah as though you see Him, for if you do not see Him, He surely sees you). The Prophet (SAW) concluded by saying: 'That was Jibril, who came to teach you your religion.'

source.name: Sahih Muslim
source.reference: Sahih Muslim Book 1, Hadith 8
